heading: Key Antiseizure Selection Tips
text: Drugs rarely, if ever, used in pediatrics: carbamazepine (Tegretol), phenytoin (Dilantin).

heading: Drug rash risk
text: Avoid if prior serious rash such as SJS/TEN, DRESS.Higher‑risk: phenytoin (DPH), carbamazepine, oxcarbazepine, lamotrigine, zonisamide, felbamate.Consider instead: levetiracetam, valproate, benzodiazepines, brivaracetam, lacosamide.

heading: Sulfonamide (“sulfa”) issues
text: Contain sulfa moiety: topiramate, zonisamide, acetazolamide.In true severe sulfonamide allergy, most avoid these and favor levetiracetam, valproate, benzodiazepines, carbamazepine/oxcarbazepine (if no serious rash history), lamotrigine with counseling, etc.

heading: Renal failure
text: Dose‑adjust / avoid accumulation.Need adjustment or extended interval: gabapentin, levetiracetam, zonisamide, topiramate.More hepatic/less renal clearance (often preferred in advanced CKD, with their own caveats): valproate, carbamazepine, oxcarbazepine, lacosamide, clobazam.

heading: Hepatic dysfunction
text: Prefer: levetiracetam, lacosamide.Avoid or use great caution: valproate, carbamazepine, phenytoin, phenobarbital, chronic benzodiazepines.

heading: Weight/metabolic concerns
text: Better: lamotrigine, levetiracetam; topiramate/zonisamide can cause weight loss but have cognitive/renal stone risks.Less ideal: valproate, carbamazepine, pregabalin.

heading: Kidney stones/metabolic acidosis
text: Avoid or monitor closely: topiramate, zonisamide, acetazolamide.Better: lamotrigine, levetiracetam, valproate.

heading: Psychiatric comorbidity
text: Mood‑friendly: lamotrigine, valproate (bipolar spectrum), clobazam.Potentially mood‑worsening: levetiracetam, brivaracetam, topiramate, phenobarbital.

heading: Women of childbearing potential
text: Prefer: lamotrigine, levetiracetam.Avoid if possible: valproate (especially generalized epilepsies), topiramate, phenytoin, phenobarbital.

heading: Generalized epilepsies
text: Use: valproate (if feasible), levetiracetam, lamotrigine, ethosuximide (absence), clobazam, topiramate/zonisamide.Avoid: carbamazepine, oxcarbazepine, phenytoin, gabapentin, tiagabine.

heading: Focal epilepsies
text: Use: carbamazepine, oxcarbazepine, lamotrigine, levetiracetam, lacosamide, cenobamate (older patients).Avoid: ethosuximide (absence‑only), vigabatrin unless specific indication.

heading: Cognitive concerns
text: Better: lamotrigine, levetiracetam, brivaracetam.Worse: topiramate, phenobarbital, chronic benzodiazepines, high‑dose zonisamide.

heading: Polypharmacy / interaction‑heavy patients
text: Preferred: levetiracetam, lacosamide, brivaracetam, gabapentin/pregabalin.Caution: carbamazepine, phenytoin, phenobarbital, primidone (inducers), valproate (inhibitor).

heading: “Special” non‑standard / adjuncts
text: Anakinra: consider in FIRES/NORSE or cytokine‑mediated epileptic encephalopathy when standard ASMs and first‑line immunotherapy fail.Steroids/immunotherapy: for suspected autoimmune epilepsy, NORSE/FIRES, or clear inflammatory evidence.Cenobamate: very effective for refractory focal‑onset seizures in adults/older teens; slow titration due to DRESS and cardiac concerns.Clinical SituationPrefer / Consider FirstAvoid / Use with CautionHistory of serious drug rash (SJS/TEN, DRESS)Levetiracetam, valproate, benzodiazepines, brivaracetam, lacosamidePhenytoin, carbamazepine, oxcarbazepine, lamotrigine

heading: Neonatal Seizure Diagnosis and Treatment – Quick Pathway
text: (Term / late‑preterm infants, <28 days CGA)

heading: 1. Suspected Seizure
text:
StepKey actions

Initial assessmentAttempt to record video if safe; note semiology and duration; document vital signs; check glucose and electrolytes per local protocol.
Emergent treatment if seizure highly suspectedGive phenobarbital (PHB) 20 mg/kg IV load (preferred) or lorazepam 0.1 mg/kg IV if PHB not immediately available. Discuss with neurology and initiate continuous EEG (cEEG) urgently.

heading: 2. Confirmation
text:
SituationManagement

Seizure confirmed on cEEG or suspicion remains very highTreat with PHB 20 mg/kg IV as first‑line antiseizure medication (counting any emergent dose toward this total). Aim for PHB level in at least the low‑ to mid‑20 µg/mL range.

heading: 3. Second‑Line Therapy
text:
StepOptions / notes

Seizures persist after first PHB load1) Give an additional 10–20 mg/kg PHB (dose guided by blood pressure and respiratory status), and/or 2) Add a second‑line agent: levetiracetam 60 mg/kg IV or fosphenytoin 20 mg/kg IV (as PE). Levetiracetam is often favored in neonates with cardiac disease; fosphenytoin may be preferred when a sodium‑channel–responsive channelopathy is suspected.

heading: 4. Third‑Line and Refractory Therapy
text:
LevelTreatment

Third lineUse whichever of levetiracetam 60 mg/kg, fosphenytoin 20 mg/kg, or lacosamide 4–8 mg/kg has not yet been given, based on local protocol and evolving safety data.
Fourth lineMidazolam: 0.2 mg/kg IV bolus, then continuous infusion at 0.2 mg/kg/h. Check EEG about every 15 minutes; if seizures continue, repeat 0.2 mg/kg bolus and increase infusion by 0.1–0.2 mg/kg/h to a maximum of 2 mg/kg/h.
If still refractory or midazolam not toleratedConsider ketamine infusion or other ICU‑level therapies after discussion with neurology/intensive care. In select refractory cases, PHB

Skin‑integrity precautionsPremature infants, those on ECMO, pharmacologically paralyzed infants, and those in incubators are at high risk for skin breakdown with prolonged electrodes; remove or reposition leads as soon as clinically safe.

heading: Acute Symptomatic Neonatal Seizures
text:
Definition: Acute symptomatic (acute provoked) neonatal seizures are electrographic seizures that occur in close temporal association with an acute brain insult or systemic disturbance such as hypoxic‑ischemic encephalopathy, stroke, intracranial hemorrhage, infection, or significant metabolic derangement, in an infant without prior epilepsy.
In current neonatal ILAE frameworks, seizures are defined electrographically: sudden, repetitive, evolving, and stereotyped EEG discharges with clear onset, evolution, and offset; clinical signs may be absent or subtle.

2 – Probable seizureClinically clear focal clonic or focal tonic seizure, or seizure confirmed on amplitude‑integrated EEG.Treat as seizure.
3 – Possible seizureEvent suspicious but not classic, or EEG correlation unclear.Consider treatment based on overall context; discuss with neurology.
4 – Suspected seizureInsufficient evidence to meet seizure criteria.Generally do not treat as seizure alone.

heading: Ketogenic Diet (KD)
text: High-fat (80–90%), adequate protein, limited carbohydrate diet used as adjunctive therapy for drug-resistant epilepsy.

heading: Indications and Efficacy
text:
StudyKey finding

Neal 2008 (randomized prospective)38% had >50% seizure reduction; 7% seizure-free (vs 6% controls, 0% seizure-free).
Johns Hopkins 19977% seizure-free; 20% with >90% reduction; 50% with >50% reduction after 1 year.
Freitas 2007 (54 children)57% achieved >75% seizure control.
Cochrane 2016Seizure freedom up to 55% and seizure reduction up to 85% in the 4:1 KD group after 3 months.

heading: Initiation (EMU or Inpatient)
text:
Monday admission (9 AM) to EMU (hospitalization per keto team and epileptologist discretion).
Patients do not need to fast for this admission.
Non-caloric beverages only on admission; formula options: RCF, Ketocal 3:1 or 4:1.
Day one fasting labs: BMP and lipids; liquid diet for first day for 100% of calories.

heading: Illness, NPO, and On-Call Management
text:
Basic principles: Maintain ketosis. Avoid carbs. Average bicarb 18–23, BS 50–80 (if on TPX/ZGN may have lower bicarb).

ScenarioRecommended action

High-carb food ingestedContinue current ratio; do not fast. Try adding extra fat (tablespoon of butter or oil for PO-fed; continue keto formula for G-tube).
Vomiting / refusing to eatGive ½ meal quantity and double the number of meals per day. If still refusing, try liquid ketogenic replacement (sip all day). If still refusing, carb replacement: 1½–2 oz OJ or apple juice up to every 4 hours. Pedialyte is OK (less carb, more volume needed). Encourag

heading: Medication Considerations
text:
Acceptable forms: Tablets, capsules, IM, IV, rectal, nasal drops, sprays, nebulizers.
Avoid: Chewables, oral suspensions/liquids/elixirs (use only if no substitute).
Acceptable fever/pain meds: Brand name Tylenol 325 mg tabs/caps, 500 mg caps; Feverall rectal suppositories (80, 120, 325 mg); Children’s Junior Motrin 100 mg caps; Advil 200 mg caps, 100 mg/5 mL (use sparingly — contains some carbs).
Addition of concomitant meds including pain management: oral meds must have minimal CHO.

heading: Vagus Nerve Stimulator (VNS)
text: VNS is not a cure. It is used to help control or lessen the severity of seizures. VNS is discussed prior to implantation at the epilepsy surgery conference to determine eligibility.

heading: Basics for Families
text:
The generator is implanted under the skin of the chest; electrodes wrap around the left vagus nerve in the neck.
A stimulus is sent to the vagus nerve at programmed intervals to attempt to prevent seizures.
Newer devices (Model 106 and Sentiva) monitor heart rate and deliver a slightly higher current for rapid heart-rate changes that may be associated with seizures.
VNS is activated just after implantation and ramped up via clinic visits and automatic protocols every 1–2 weeks.
Battery lasts 6–10 years; generator must be surgically replaced. The lead on the vagus nerve is generally not removed

heading: Using the Magnet
text:
Swipe the magnet over the generator for a count of 3 seconds.
Once the magnet is removed, the generator provides a higher-amplitude pulse for ~60 seconds to try to abort the seizure or shorten the postictal period.
The magnet can never be used too much or damage the device. The VNS can only be activated every 60 seconds regardless of how many times you swipe.

heading: Side Effects and Temporary Shut-Off
text:
Side effects: Local discomfort, throat pain, cough, changes in voice pitch/volume, reflux. These can usually be modulated by adjusting VNS settings.
OSA can be an adverse effect — increased daytime sleepiness or worsening snoring should be evaluated with PSG.
To temporarily turn off: Place the magnet over the generator and tape it in place with heavy tape. As long as the magnet is over the generator, no current is delivered. Remove the magnet to resume normal stimulation.

heading: Imaging and Surgery Considerations
text:
The VNS lead is ferromagnetic and may heat up in a strong magnetic field. MRI should not be performed of the chest and upper spine.
Before MRI: set output current to zero (can be done by EEG techs/APPs in EMU for inpatients or clinic RNs for outpatients). Restore settings after scan.
Electrocautery during surgery can affect the device. Interrogate before and after surgery to ensure proper function. The OR typically requests the device be turned off before surgery and back on after.

heading: Overview
text: Infantile spasms (IS) is an epilepsy of early infancy, affecting 3-4.5/10,000 live births, with typical onset 1-15 months (peak 4-9 months), M>F.  Often associated with developmental issues.Patients can have flexor, extensor, mixed flexor-extensor, and/or asymmetric spasms.  IS can mimic reflux or myoclonus.  Patients are often admitted for continuous EEG to definitively evaluate for infantile spasms.

heading: Infantile Spasms — Prognosis
text: Resolution of spasms is associated with better developmental outcomes. The primary driving factor is treatment with a standard first-line therapy (ACTH, prednisolone, or vigabatrin).In children with known etiology, prognosis is related to etiology. Children without prior developmental delay or prior seizures may have a better prognosis.50–60% develop other seizure types in the future; about 35% develop Lennox–Gastaut syndrome.

heading: Infantile Spasms — Epidemiology
text:
FeatureDetails

Incidence≈2–3 per 10,000 live births.
Usual age of onset4–9 months (almost all before 1 year).
SexSlight male predominance.

heading: Infantile Spasms — Etiology
text:
CategoryNotes / examples

Known cause (~70%)Perinatal HIE, neurocutaneous disorders, brain malformations, chromosomal abnormalities, inborn errors of metabolism (>200 causes).
Unknown cause (~30%)No clear etiology despite evaluation.
Selected predisposing conditionsAicardi syndrome; CMV; hemimegalencephaly; HIE; incontinentia pigmenti; lissencephaly; Sturge–Weber; PKU; TSC; trisomy 21; ARX‑related disorders.

heading: Who to Monitor and How
text:
ItemDetails

Applies toInfants with IS treated with ACTH or prednisolone (not vigabatrin alone).
BP monitoringMeasure BP 2–3 times per week (upper extremity preferred). Ensure infant is calm; repeat readings and average if elevated.
Initial “elevated” threshold>95th percentile for age/sex/height or >102/62 in infants <1 year.

heading: Stepwise Approach
text:
Decision pointAction

BP elevated at visitRepeat measurements during visit; if average remains elevated, recheck in 24 hours.
Still elevated at 24‑hour recheckRecheck again in 24 hours (third check within 72 hours).
Sustained hypertension (>95th percentile or >102/62 on 3 checks)Obtain BMP; obtain echocardiogram for infants on ACTH; start daily BP monitoring until normalized or management plan set.

heading: Core Concepts
text: Seizures  Seizures result from abnormal excessive rhythmic neurologic electrical activity. This abnormal activity can occur in both hemispheres (generalized seizure) or within one hemisphere (focal seizure), which may spread to the entire brain. Generalized tonic-clonic seizures – motions of which consist of a tonic phase with muscle stiffening, followed by a clonic phase with rhythmic muscle contractions. Focal seizures more often occur in the setting of cerebral insult. Unlike generalized seizures, symptoms of focal seizures vary according to the anatomic location of the abnormal electrical

heading: SUDEP – Sudden Unexpected Death in Epilepsy
text: Definition: Sudden, unexpected, non‑traumatic, non‑drowning death in a person with epilepsy, with or without a witnessed seizure, and no structural or toxicologic cause identified at autopsy.

heading: How Common Is SUDEP?
text:
(Approximate, order‑of‑magnitude figures for counseling.)

PopulationApproximate incidence

Children with epilepsy≈0.2–0.5 per 1000 patient‑years (about 1 per 2,000–5,000 children per year).
Adults with epilepsy≈1–2 per 1000 patient‑years (about 1 per 1,000 per year).
Drug‑resistant / surgery‑candidate cohortsUp to ~9 per 1000 patient‑years in some series.
Dravet syndrome / SCN1A‑related epilepsyCumulative SUDEP risk substantially higher; older cohorts estimate several to ~10–20% across childhood and young adulthood.

heading: Major Risk Factors
text:
(Focus on modifiable items)

Risk factorPractical takeaway

Frequent generalized tonic–clonic seizures (especially ≥3/year)Strongest known risk factor; reducing GTCS frequency is the main protective strategy.
Nocturnal GTCS and sleeping aloneMany SUDEPs occur at night; consider room‑sharing, reliable monitors, or seizure‑alert devices in high‑risk patients.
Poor adherence or abrupt ASM withdrawalReview adherence at each visit; warn against suddenly stopping medications.
Long duration of active epilepsy, drug resistance, polytherapyMarkers of severe disease; refer to epilepsy center for surgery

heading: Counseling Script (At‑a‑Glance)
text:
“There is a small but real risk of sudden death related to epilepsy called SUDEP. In children, it may be as high as about 1 in 2–3 thousand each year in some groups.”
“Risk is highest in people who have frequent big convulsive seizures, especially at night. The main goal is to reduce or eliminate those seizures.”
“Taking medicine every day, avoiding missed doses, treating clusters quickly, and having some form of nighttime supervision or alarm all help lower risk.”
“This topic should be revisited over time, especially as adolescents gain independence.”

heading: VPA-Induced Hyperammonemic Encephalopathy
text: Can occur even with therapeutic VPA levels. Suspect in any patient on VPA with acute confusion, lethargy, vomiting, or worsening seizures.

heading: Recognition
text:
Check ammonia level in any encephalopathic patient on valproic acid — even if VPA level is "therapeutic."

Ammonia > 80 µmol/L — clinically significant
Ammonia > 100 µmol/L — concerning, likely symptomatic
Ammonia > 200 µmol/L — critical, risk of cerebral edema

Also check: hepatic function panel (AST, ALT, total/direct bilirubin), VPA level, CBC with platelets, lipase

heading: L-Carnitine Treatment Protocol
text:
Indication: VPA-induced hyperammonemia with encephalopathy, VPA overdose, or hepatotoxicity.
IV Levocarnitine (Carnitor):

Loading dose: 100 mg/kg IV (max 6 g) over 30 minutes
Maintenance: 50 mg/kg IV (max 3 g) every 8 hours
Continue until ammonia normalizes and mental status improves
Transition to oral: 50-100 mg/kg/day divided TID (max 3 g/day)

Additional management:

Hold VPA immediately
Consider lactulose if ammonia critically elevated (> 200 µmol/L)
Monitor ammonia every 4-6 hours until trending down
If on VPA + other enzyme inducers (phenytoin, carbamazepine, phenobarbital) → higher

heading: VPA Overdose
text:
VPA level > 150 mg/L: Significant toxicity likely — L-carnitine, supportive care
VPA level > 300 mg/L: Severe — consider hemodialysis (VPA is protein-bound at therapeutic levels but saturation occurs at high concentrations, making dialysis more effective)
VPA level > 850 mg/L: Life-threatening — hemodialysis strongly recommended
Activated charcoal if within 1-2 hours of ingestion (VPA is well-absorbed)
No specific antidote — L-carnitine is the primary treatment adjunct

heading: Non‑Epileptic Paroxysmal Events of Early Childhood
Overview
text: "Funny" or "weird" spells are among the most common child neurology consults and are frequently nonepileptic. In infants and toddlers, the most common benign causes are primary motor stereotypies, shuddering attacks, and breath‑holding spells, but a broad range of non‑epileptic paroxysms can mimic seizures or syncope. Key questions: trigger, awareness, duration, pattern repeatability, and video review.

heading: Primary Motor Stereotypies
text:
Onset: infancy/early childhood (often 6–24 months).
Phenomenology: brief, repetitive, patterned movements (e.g., hand flapping, arm waving, head nodding, body rocking, leg kicking).
Typical features:

Last 1–2 seconds up to several seconds; often occur in clusters.
No alteration in awareness; child can be distracted or interrupted.
Occur during excitement, anticipation, engrossment (videos, music, feeding, high chair, car seat, activity center).
Same movement every time; non‑evolving pattern over months.
Can have dystonic character.

Course: often persist but may lessen; seen in typical develo

heading: Shuddering Attacks
text:
Age: usually 4–12 months.
Phenomenology: sudden brief "shiver" or tremor of head/shoulders/trunk, often with facial grimace or arm stiffening; parents describe "like ice‑cold water poured down the neck."
Duration: 1–2 seconds; can recur many times daily.
Awareness: intact; child continues activity; no postictal state.
Triggers: excitement, feeding, frustration, or spontaneous.
Course: benign; typically improve with age.
Management: reassurance; no routine EEG or imaging when history and exam are typical.

heading: Breath‑Holding Spells (BHS)
text: Breath‑holding spells are brief, non‑epileptic events in young children where emotion or pain triggers apnea, color change, and transient loss of consciousness with full recovery. Cyanotic spells follow crying/anger with forced expiration, cyanosis, and brief limpness or stiffening; pallid (reflex anoxic) spells follow sudden pain or fright with marked pallor and syncope‑like collapse. Spells usually start between 6–18 months and remit by early school age without long‑term neurologic sequelae. Mechanism is a brainstem/autonomic reflex (inspiratory arrest or vagally mediated asystole), often wo

heading: Paroxysmal Kinesigenic Dyskinesia (PKD)
text:
Age: typically school‑age through adolescence.
Phenomenology: brief attacks (seconds to <1 minute) of dystonia, chorea, or mixed dyskinesia (often unilateral or asymmetric); described as "twisting," "jerking," or "locking up."
Trigger: sudden voluntary movement or start (standing up quickly, taking first step, running, sudden direction change); often many attacks per day.
Awareness: preserved; no postictal state.
Between attacks: normal neurologic and developmental exam.
Course: often familial; commonly associated with PRRT2 variants; excellent response to low‑dose carbamazepine/oxcarbazepi

heading: Primary Motor Stereotypies vs Tics
text:
Stereotypies

Onset: infancy/early childhood (<3 years).
Movements: rhythmic, patterned, prolonged (e.g., hand flapping, rocking).
Triggers: excitement, engrossment; often when sitting or standing still.
Awareness: intact; distraction can interrupt behavior.
Course: long‑standing but non‑progressive.

Tics

Onset: usually >4–5 years.
Movements: brief, non‑rhythmic, can migrate (eye blinks, throat clears, shoulder shrugs, etc.).
Premonitory urge and relief after tic; suppression possible but uncomfortable.
Course: waxing and waning over weeks–months.

heading: Sandifer Syndrome
text:
Age: infancy/early childhood, often 2–18 months.
Phenomenology: episodic, often dramatic arching and dystonic posturing of neck and trunk (opisthotonus, torticollis‑like), commonly around feeds.
Association: gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD) and/or hiatal hernia; may have poor weight gain, irritability, or feeding aversion.
Features suggesting Sandifer rather than seizure:

Temporal relationship to feeding or supine positioning after feeds.
Child usually alert and interactive between spells.
Normal EEG.

Management: reflux measures (thickened feeds, positioning, acid suppression when appr

heading: Benign Paroxysmal Vertigo of Childhood (BPVC)
text:
Age: typically 2–6 years.
Phenomenology: sudden episodes of vertigo/unsteadiness; child may grab caregiver, appear frightened, or fall; may have nystagmus, pallor, vomiting.
Duration: minutes, then complete recovery.
Association: strong personal/family history of migraine; often evolves into migraine later.
Work‑up: normal neurologic exam between attacks; if atypical or persistent ataxia, perform MRI ± vestibular evaluation.
Management: reassurance; treat as migraine variant if frequent (lifestyle, prophylaxis).

heading: Benign Paroxysmal Torticollis of Infancy
text:
Age: onset usually 3–8 months, resolving by 3–5 years.
Phenomenology: recurrent episodes of head tilt (often alternating sides) with pallor, irritability, vomiting, or ataxia.
Duration: hours to days; normal baseline between episodes.
Association: migraine spectrum; many later develop migraine.
Work‑up: neurologic and ocular exam; imaging if baseline torticollis persists, there are focal deficits, or course is atypical.
Management: reassurance; treat associated conditions (reflux, migraine triggers); PT if persistent neck tightness.

heading: Spasmus Nutans
text:
Age: typically 4–18 months.
Triad:

Fine, high‑frequency (often asymmetric) nystagmus.
Head nodding.
Intermittent torticollis.

Course: generally improves and resolves by age 3–4 years.
Critical consideration: optic pathway or other intracranial lesions can mimic this presentation.
Work‑up: detailed ophthalmologic exam; MRI brain and orbits with contrast if atypical age, visual symptoms, optic disc abnormalities, or persistent/progressive signs.
Management: if imaging and ophthalmology are normal, reassure and monitor.

heading: Self‑Gratification Behaviors (Infantile Masturbation)
text:
Age: approximately 6 months–5 years.
Phenomenology: episodes of quiet staring or grunting with rhythmic pelvic or thigh adduction, leg stiffening, or rubbing against surfaces; often facial flushing, sweating.
Clues:

Characteristic posture (prone, or sitting with tightly adducted legs).
Awareness preserved; episodes can be interrupted or distracted, but child may appear frustrated when interrupted.
No postictal state; occur in specific settings (car seat, crib, high chair).

Management: explain benign nature and non‑epileptic mechanism; reassure caregivers; avoid shaming; encourage gentle redi

heading: Night Terrors / Confusional Arousals vs Nocturnal Seizures
text:

Duration: minutes; resolves with return to sleep.
Morning: amnesia for the event.
Management: sleep hygiene, scheduled awakenings if frequent; consider sleep study when atypical.

Studies indicate that approximately 20% of patients presenting for evaluation of seizure are misdiagnosed as having epilepsy. Conditions most commonly mistaken for epileptiform seizure activity include syncope and psychogenic non-epileptic seizures.

heading: Thunderclap Headache
text:
Definition: Severe headache reaching maximum intensity within 60 seconds ("worst headache of my life").

Must rule out subarachnoid hemorrhage (SAH):

Step 1: Non-contrast CT head — sensitivity ~98% within 6 hours of onset, drops to ~93% at 24h and ~50% by day 5
Step 2: If CT negative and within 2 weeks of onset → lumbar puncture looking for xanthochromia (send tube 4 for spectrophotometry) and elevated RBCs that do not clear
Step 3: If LP also negative → consider CTA or MRA to evaluate for unruptured aneurysm, cerebral venous sinus thrombosis (CVST), or reversible cerebral vasoconstriction sy

heading: Headache Red Flags — "SNOOP" Mnemonic
text:
S — Systemic symptoms (fever, weight loss, rash) or Secondary risk factors (immunocompromised, malignancy, coagulopathy)
N — Neurologic signs (papilledema, focal deficits, altered mental status, meningismus)
O — Onset sudden (thunderclap) or recent onset in child < 5 years
O — Other associated features: positional (worse lying down → IIH; worse standing → low-pressure), nocturnal or early morning awakening headaches, triggered by Valsalva/cough/exertion
P — Prior headache history: new headache type, pattern change, progressive worsening, refractory to appropriate treatment

heading: When to Image a Pediatric Headache
text:
Urgent imaging (CT head) indicated if:

Thunderclap onset
Focal neurologic deficits
Papilledema or loss of venous pulsations on fundoscopy
Altered mental status or worsening GCS
Signs of increased ICP: vomiting (especially AM), personality change, head tilt
Post-traumatic with "red flag" features
VP shunt concern

MRI brain (outpatient) consider if:

Headache always same side
Headache wakes from sleep or present on awakening
Progressive headache over weeks with new neurologic findings
Age < 5 with new recurrent headaches
Neurofibromatosis (screen for optic pathway glioma)

heading: Neuroembryology and Developmental Timing
text:
Understanding when in development a process occurs is the conceptual foundation for why malformations look the way they do. The brain builds itself in a strictly ordered sequence — each stage depends on the fidelity of the stage before it. Disruption at a given developmental window produces a characteristic pattern of malformation that is largely predictable from timing alone.
Two principles follow from this: (1) earlier disruption produces more severe and widespread malformation; (2) the imaging pattern of a malformation reflects its developmental stage of origin — recognizing where in the se

text:
Craniosynostosis — premature fusion of one or more cranial sutures — produces characteristic head shape abnormalities and carries risk of elevated intracranial pressure, especially in multi-suture and syndromic forms. heading: Conceptual Framework
text: Most neurometabolic disorders can be organized along two fundamental axes: biochemical class (what is biochemically wrong) and clinical tempo (how they present over time). Overlaid on this is the question of which organ systems are involved and what the MRI shows. Getting these three axes right guides both the urgency and the direction of your workup.

heading: The Core Dichotomy: Small-Molecule vs. Large-Molecule
text:
Small-molecule (intoxication/energy) disorders involve the accumulation of toxic small metabolites or failure of energy production. Because these metabolites are water-soluble and can be cleared — by dialysis, dietary manipulation, cofactors, or specific medications — many are highly treatable. This is the group you cannot miss.
Large-molecule (organelle) disorders involve failure to process complex molecules within organelles (lysosomes, peroxisomes, Golgi). Damage accumulates structurally over time. With limited exceptions (early enzyme replacement, HSC transplant in selected cases), these a

heading: A Critical Nuance: Intoxication vs. Energy Failure
text: Within the small-molecule category, intoxication-type disorders (e.g., organic acidemias, UCDs, MSUD) and energy-failure disorders (e.g., FAODs, mitochondrial RC defects, GLUT1) are distinct. This matters clinically: organic acidemias cause toxicity from accumulating organic acids and often produce ketosis and metabolic acidosis; FAODs primarily cause energy failure during fasting and produce hypoketotic hypoglycemia. Mitochondrial disorders can present with either pattern or both. Recognizing the sub-type guides initial empirical management.

heading: Why Some IEMs Cause Episodic Disease and Others Cause Steady Decline
text: This depends on whether the pathological mechanism is acute metabolite accumulation (reversible if treated) vs. structural or irreversible cellular damage over time. In UCD or MSUD, ammonia or leucine rises during metabolic stress, causing encephalopathy — but the neuron itself can recover if the crisis resolves. In contrast, lysosomal storage with progressive lipid or substrate deposition is structural; neurons die. Many disorders are intermediate: basal ganglia injury in organic acidemias (irreversible stroke-like damage) can occur against a backdrop of episodic presentation.

heading: Phenotypic Spectrum and Residual Enzyme Activity
text:
The same gene can produce wildly different phenotypes depending on residual enzyme activity or variant severity. Classic PKU with near-zero PAH activity causes severe untreated intellectual disability; mild hyperphenylalaninemia from a different variant may be asymptomatic. Infantile Pompe (GAA) causes fatal cardiomyopathy in infancy; late-onset Pompe causes a slowly progressive limb-girdle/respiratory myopathy in adulthood. Residents should resist anchoring to the textbook presentation of a named disorder — the phenotypic range of most IEMs is broader than the classic description.

heading: Mitochondrial Disorders
text:
Maternal (mtDNA), autosomal recessive, or autosomal dominant inheritance.
Multisystem involvement; neurologic plus myopathy, cardiomyopathy/arrhythmia, endocrine, GI, renal disease.
Triggers: fever, fasting, infection, some medications (e.g., valproate in suspected POLG disease).
Testing: lactate ± pyruvate, CK, MRI, mtDNA/nuclear panels, RC chain analysis ± biopsy.
Management: avoid mitochondrial toxins; supportive care; CoQ10 or targeted therapies in specific deficiencies.

heading: Developmental Delay ± Epilepsy – Genetic and Metabolic Work‑up
Step 1 – Clinical Triage
text:
History: prenatal/perinatal events; regression vs static course; seizure types; family history.
Exam: distinctive features (dysmorphism, neurocutaneous signs), head size, organomegaly, movement disorder.
MRI brain when focal deficits, abnormal head growth, refractory epilepsy, or concerning exam.

heading: Step 2 – Broad Genetic Testing
text:
If the child meets criteria for global developmental delay (≥2 domains, moderate/severe) with any combination of ID, ASD, epilepsy, abnormal exam, or regression/evolving picture, or is not responding to standard treatment:

Order trio WES/WGS as the primary test.
Add targeted or syndrome‑specific panels only when clearly indicated.

heading: Step 3 – Additional Studies and Follow‑up
text:
Chromosomal microarray (if not already done).
Fragile X testing (especially males or suggestive family history).
Metabolic testing that may have been missed, guided by phenotype.
Consider EEG to evaluate for ESES or other epileptic encephalopathy patterns.
Consider repeat MRI if concern for evolving leukodystrophy or basal‑ganglia changes.
Refer to a Neurogenetics clinic when expedited, comprehensive evaluation or VUS resolution is needed.

heading: Genetic Counseling, Broad Testing, and GINA
Why Genetic Counseling Is Needed
text:
Broad testing (WES/WGS, large panels) should be ordered with pre‑test counseling by a genetics professional.
Counseling covers possible outcomes (positive, negative, VUS), test limitations, family implications, and the chance of unexpected findings (secondary findings, consanguinity, non‑paternity).

heading: Result Types
text:
Positive – pathogenic/likely pathogenic variant explaining the phenotype; guides management and family testing.
Negative – no causative variant found; a genetic cause may still exist.
Variant of uncertain significance (VUS) – uncertain; should not usually change management. Most reclassified VUS move downward toward benign/likely benign rather than toward pathogenic.

heading: GINA – Key Points for Families (U.S.)
text:
GINA limits how health insurers and most employers can use genetic information.
Health insurers cannot use genetic test results to set eligibility or premiums or require testing.
Employers with ≥15 employees cannot use genetic information in hiring, firing, or promotion decisions.
GINA does not cover life, disability, or long‑term‑care insurance, and does not apply to very small employers; families should understand these limits when deciding about testing.

heading: Autoimmune Encephalitis — Treatment:
text: Acute: Typically includes high dose methylprednisolone followed by IVIG or PLEX (3-5 days). heading: Diagnostic Workup
text:
Lumbar Puncture:

Classic finding: albuminocytologic dissociation (elevated protein, normal WBC < 10/µL)
CSF protein may be normal in the first week — sensitivity improves after day 7
If WBC > 50/µL → reconsider diagnosis (think infectious, HIV, Lyme, CMV)

NCS/EMG:

May be normal in first 1-2 weeks
Optimal timing: 2-3 weeks after symptom onset
Early findings: prolonged F-waves, prolonged distal latencies, absent H-reflexes
Helps classify subtype: AIDP (demyelinating, most common) vs AMAN/AMSAN (axonal)

Additional labs: Anti-ganglioside antibodies (anti-GM1, anti-GD1a, anti-GQ1b for Mil

heading: Respiratory Monitoring — "20/30/40 Rule"
text:
Monitor FVC (forced vital capacity) every 4-6 hours in all GBS patients:

FVC < 20 mL/kg → consider ICU transfer / intubation
NIF (MIP) < -30 cmH₂O → respiratory muscles weakening
FVC declining > 30% from baseline → rapid progression, prepare for intubation

Other intubation indicators: inability to count to 20 in one breath, paradoxical abdominal breathing, oxygen desaturation, inability to lift head off bed
Do NOT rely on pulse oximetry alone — desaturation is a LATE finding in neuromuscular respiratory failure.

heading: Treatment Protocol
text:
IVIG (first-line in pediatrics):

Dose: 2 g/kg total, given over 2-5 days (e.g., 0.4 g/kg/day × 5 days)
Check IgA level before first dose (IgA deficiency → anaphylaxis risk)
Monitor for adverse effects: headache, aseptic meningitis, renal dysfunction, thrombosis

Plasmapheresis (PLEX):

Alternative to IVIG — equally effective in severe cases
Typically 5 exchanges over 1-2 weeks
Preferred if IVIG contraindicated or patient not improving
Do NOT give IVIG after PLEX (PLEX removes the IVIG)

Steroids are NOT effective in GBS (unlike CIDP)

heading: ICP Management Protocol
text:
Elevated ICP: ICP > 20-25 mmHg sustained for > 10 minutes. Goal ICP < 20 mmHg.
Clinical signs: Headache, vomiting, vision changes, altered mental status, pupillary changes. Cushing's triad (hypertension + bradycardia + irregular breathing) is a LATE sign suggesting impending herniation.
CPP = MAP − ICP (target age-appropriate CPP)

heading: Step 1: Immediate Measures
text:
Notify neurosurgery — NSGY typically aids in ICP management
Optimize sedation and pain control
Head of bed elevated to 30°, head and neck midline (optimize venous drainage)
Hyperventilate to PaCO₂ 32-35 mmHg (temporary bridge only — do not maintain)
If EVD/ICP drain present: unclamp, set at 15 mmHg, notify NSGY
See Pupillometry section for noninvasive ICP trending (NPI < 3 = pathological)

heading: Step 2: First-Line — Hypertonic Saline
text:
3% Hypertonic Saline (HTS) — 0.5 mEq/mL Na⁺

Dose: 5 mL/kg IV bolus via peripheral IV or central line
May repeat if ICP remains elevated
Hold if serum Na⁺ ≥ 165 mmol/L
Monitor serum sodium every 4-6 hours

heading: Step 3: Second-Line — Mannitol
text:
Use if fluid overloaded or not responsive to HTS:

Dose: Mannitol 0.5-1 g/kg IV bolus
Hold if serum osmolarity ≥ 340 mOsm/kg
May repeat after 15 minutes if ICP remains elevated
Monitor serum osmolarity, electrolytes, and volume status

heading: Step 4: Refractory ICP
text:
If ICP remains elevated despite Steps 1-3:

Neuromuscular blockade (eliminates shivering, coughing, ventilator dyssynchrony)
Deep sedation:
  
  Ketamine + midazolam infusion, OR
  Pentobarbital coma — titrate to burst suppression on cEEG
  

Therapeutic hypothermia to 32-34°C
Neurosurgical intervention: decompressive craniectomy, hemicraniectomy

heading: Vision Loss
text: Key definitions for optic disc findings:Papillitis - Optic disc inflammation. May be unilateral. Papilledema - Optic disc edema specifically due to increased intracranial pressure. It is most often bilateral. Retrobulbar optic neuritis - Optic neuritis involving the optic nerve behind the globe. Depending on which part of the optic nerve is involved, papillitis may not be present. Neuroretinitis - Inflammatory process involving the optic discs with exudative changes in the nerve fiber layer of the retina (macular star). This is not associated with demyelinating disease.

heading: Approach to Unequal Pupils
text:
Key question: Which pupil is abnormal — the large one or the small one?


EMERGENCY: Large pupil + ptosis + eye "down and out" = CN III palsy until proven otherwise. In a post-operative or head-injured patient, this may indicate uncal herniation requiring emergent neurosurgical evaluation.

heading: Step 1: Examine in Light vs Dark
text:
Anisocoria greater in light (large pupil doesn't constrict) → problem is with the large pupil
  
  CN III palsy (check for ptosis, eye movement limitation)
  Pharmacologic mydriasis (topical atropine/cyclopentolate — won't constrict to 1% pilocarpine)
  Traumatic mydriasis (iris sphincter damage)
  Tonic pupil (Adie) — vermiform movements, slow constriction, constricts to dilute 0.1% pilocarpine (denervation supersensitivity)
  

Anisocoria greater in dark (small pupil doesn't dilate) → problem is with the small pupil
  
  Horner syndrome (ptosis + miosis ± anhidrosis)
  Pharmacologic miosis

heading: Horner Syndrome Workup
text:
Confirm: Topical 4% cocaine or 0.5% apraclonidine — Horner pupil fails to dilate with cocaine (or reverses with apraclonidine).
Localize:

Central (1st order): Stroke, demyelination, tumor — MRI brain + cervical spine
Preganglionic (2nd order): Pancoast tumor, neuroblastoma (pediatric!), carotid dissection — CT/MRI chest + neck
Postganglionic (3rd order): Carotid dissection, cavernous sinus lesion — MRA head/neck


Pediatric Horner: Must rule out neuroblastoma (obtain urine catecholamines — HVA, VMA) and birth-related brachial plexus injury in infants. New-onset Horner in any age → imaging man

heading: CN III Palsy — Surgical vs Medical
text:
"Surgical" (pupil-involving): Mydriasis + ptosis + limited adduction/elevation/depression → concern for posterior communicating artery (PComm) aneurysm compression. Urgent CTA/MRA.
"Medical" (pupil-sparing): Ptosis + eye movement limitation but normal pupil → microvascular (diabetes, hypertension). More common in adults; rare in children.
In children: Pupil-sparing distinction is less reliable. Any CN III palsy in a child warrants imaging.

heading: Reading a Pediatric Brain MRI
text: Pediatric neuroimaging differs from adult neuroimaging in one fundamental respect: the brain is a moving target. Myelination, cortical thickness, gray-white differentiation, and ventricular size all change dramatically across the first two years of life. Every MRI must be interpreted relative to the child's age — a finding that is pathological at 18 months may be normal at 2 weeks, and vice versa.

heading: Leukodystrophy Pattern Recognition
text:
Leukodystrophies are genetically determined disorders of white matter — distinguished from acquired white matter disease by their symmetric, progressive, and anatomically patterned involvement. The single most useful first step is to identify the predominant WM pattern, which narrows the differential substantially before genetic testing is sent. The key distinction is between demyelination (myelin actively destroyed — typically progressive with MRI change) and hypomyelination (myelin never formed normally — T2-bright WM that does not progress on serial imaging).
Key Questions When Approaching

heading: Malformations of Cortical Development (MCD)
text:
MCDs arise from disruption of the three sequential stages of cortical formation: proliferation (germinal zone, weeks 7–16), migration (radial/tangential, weeks 12–24), and organization/postmigration (synaptogenesis, weeks 24–postnatal). The earlier the disruption, the more severe and widespread the malformation. This mechanistic framework is worth knowing because it maps onto the imaging phenotype: migration defects produce heterotopia and lissencephaly; organization defects produce PMG and FCD.

heading: Neonatal Brain Injury Patterns
text:
Neonatal brain injury patterns reflect the interaction between the type and duration of the insult and the regional metabolic vulnerability at the gestational age of injury. Term and preterm brains are injured differently — because their most metabolically active and most vulnerable regions differ. Understanding this mapping between insult type, gestational age, and imaging pattern is the conceptual foundation for interpreting neonatal MRI.

heading: Radiation Exposure
text: Radiation dose is measured by millisievers (mSv). Naturally occurring “background” radiation exposure is estimated to be 3 mSv per year.  At higher elevations (e.g. Denver, ~5,280 ft), there is about 1.5 mSv more per year than living at sea level. Round trip flight from coast to coast is 0.03 mSv. CT Head has 2 mSv which equates to 8 months of exposure to naturally occurring radiation. Additional lifetime risk of fatal cancer from examination is thought to be very low (1 in 100,000 to1 in 10,000)CT Head with and without is 4 mSv. Additional risk is low.
